There's a good example of how admitting to a weakness first can establish your credibility and your consequent effectiveness. It comes from the work of my associate, Dr. Gregory Neidert, who was advising a charitable organization during the recession of 2008 to 2010. They had asked for his help to get more current and prospective donors to contribute. Their existing message to such donors was, The impact of your donation has never been greater than it is today. But we know how difficult it is for many people to give during these difficult economic times. Dr. Neidert suggested they keep the same message with a slight modification, in which the weakness for the charity went first, so that the revised appeal read, We know how difficult it is for many people to give during these difficult economic times. But the impact of your donation has never been greater than it is today. The results were remarkable. The revised appeal resulted in 36% more donations. Notice how many words were changed to produce this effect? None. The only thing that changed was the sequence in which the components of the message were delivered. The weakness came first, establishing the charity's trustworthiness before delivering their crucial assertion about the impact of donations. Here's another case of a small change making a big difference in a message's persuasive impact provided that change activates or amplifies a big driver of human behavior, such as the perception of credibility.
